name: Acute stroke phase 1 mitochondrial transplantation reported no significant adverse events
summary: The first human-brain mitochondrial transplantation report supports acute procedural feasibility and safety, not efficacy.
statement: In a phase 1 acute ischemic stroke study, four subjects received autologous mitochondrial transplantation during mechanical thrombectomy, and the publication reported no significant adverse events with safety outcomes comparable to matched controls.
population_or_model: Adults with acute ischemic stroke undergoing mechanical thrombectomy; treated participants ranged from 43 to 80 years old
time_horizon: Acute procedural and early safety monitoring
quantitative_note: The full-text report states that four AIS subjects with MCA occlusion underwent transplantation and that no periprocedural, post-procedure, systemic, or access-site complications were reported.
caveats: The trial was small, early phase, and designed around safety and feasibility.
The result does not show aging-relevant efficacy, durable neurological benefit, or healthy-aging benefit.
The acute endovascular workflow may not generalize to chronic mitochondrial dysfunction or LEV-relevant settings.
